---
title: "Predicting Wildfire Intensity and Locations"
subtitle: "Cristian Castellanos, Filip Sakellariou 4/30/26"
format: revealjs
---

# Motivation

## Why Wildfires?

::: {.notes}
Climate change is one of those really big issues that everyone agrees needs to be solved but no one knows how to. So we started looking for topics and datasets where we could perform some meaningful analysis to mitigate some of its impacts. And so we found a comprehensive wildfire dataset.

While many of us tend to think of climate change as a distant issue, this hits close to home. The image here [points to image] is from a 2025 wildfire in Chalkidiki Greece that was just 300 yards away from the cottage house by grandparents had built.
:::

<!-- https://www.keeptalkinggreece.com/2025/07/02/halkidiki-vourvoourou-wildfire-village-forest/ -->

![Wildfire in Chalkidiki 2025 (image from Keep Talking Greece) ](chalkidiki_wildfire_2025.png)

## Understanding Wildfires

1. A wilfire is "[A]ny fire started by an unplanned ignition [...]; unauthorized activity; or accidental, human-caused actions, or a prescribed fire that has developed into a wildfire." [1]
2. "[T]here is general consensus that fire occurrence will increase with climate change" [2]
    <!-- USGS (US Geological Survey) is part of the Department of the Interior) -->
3. In 2024, 57,962 fires were ignited by humans (especially California) and 6,935 by humans (common in Alaska) [3]
    <!-- National Interagency Fire Center -->

::: aside

According to [1] [EPA](https://www.epa.gov/wildfires/wildland-fires-and-public-health-effects), [2] [USGS](https://www.usgs.gov/faqs/will-global-warming-produce-more-frequent-and-more-intense-wildfires), [3] [NIFC](https://www.nifc.gov/fire-information/statistics) (accessed on 4/28/2026).
:::

## Wildfire Impacts

![Illustration showing that wildfires cause frequent disruptions, increase health risks, have significant economic costs and jeopardize water and food security.](wildfire_impacts.png){width=90%}


::: aside
According to [U.S. Geographic Survey](https://www.usgs.gov/news/featured-story/good-bad-ugly-how-wildfires-reshape-landscapes) (accessed 4/29/2026). Images composed from sources [a](https://www.vecteezy.com/vector-art/24453420-vector-illustration-of-a-man-coughing), [b](
https://www.vecteezy.com/vector-art/23880460-cost-reduction-vector-illustration-with-decrease-price-minimising-or-falling-rate-of-profit-in-business-flat-cartoon-hand-drawn-templates), [c](https://www.dreamstime.com/illustration/outage-rgb-color-icon.html) and [d](
https://www.freepik.com/free-photos-vectors/water-scarcity-illustration).
:::

::: {.notes}
1. More frequent disruptions
    <!-- E.g. power outage -->
2. Health risks
    <!-- Smoke and air pollution -->
3. Economic costs
    <!-- Suppression cost, evacuation cost, etc. -->
4. Water and Food Security
    <!-- Damage to watersheds & nearby agricultural areas -->
:::

## Goal

Predict wildfire intensity and understand prevelant locations given meteorological data at a given geographic location in the continental U.S.

# Data

## Kaggle Dataset

1. US Wildfires 2014-2025
2. Combines meteorological records from [gridMET](https://www.climatologylab.org/gridmet.html) with positive wildfire ignition events provided by [IRWIN](https://www.wildfire.gov/application/irwin-integrated-reporting-wildfire-information)

![Each sample contains daily meteorological 60 days pre-ignition; during the fire; 14 days post-ignition.](data_window.png){width=80%}


::: aside

Image composed from sources [a](https://www.dreamstime.com/illustration/sun-heat.html), [b](https://www.freepik.com/premium-vector/create-minimal-simple-fire-flames-vector-art-illustration-white-background-16_251705378.htm) and [c](https://pngtree.com/freepng/ash-clipart-illustration-of-a-tree-with-dirt-on-top-of-it-cartoon-vector_11059972.html).
:::

::: {.notes}
Each sample corresponds to a 75-day window: 60 days pre-ignition ("Wildfire" column labeled "No"), day of ignition (labeled "Yes"), and 14 days post-ignition (labeled "No"). 
:::

## Key Variables

::: {style="font-size: 40%;"}

| Name | Description |
| ---- | ----------- | 
| Precipitation (mm/day) | Total daily precipitation; reduce wildfire risk. |
| Maximum Relative Humidity (%) | Highest daily relative humidity; reduces fire intensity.  |
| Minimum Relative Humidity (%) | Lowest daily relative humidity; increases spread potential |
| Specific Humidity  (kg/kg) | Mass of water vapor per unit mass of air (atmospheric moisture content independent of temperature). |
| Solar Radiation (W/m^2) | Incoming solar energy at the surface; increases ignition risk.                                    |
| Minimum Temperature (°C) | Lowest daily air temperature (affects overnight fuel-moisture renetion). |
| Maximum Temperature  (°C) | Highest daily air temperature; increases ignition risk. |
| Wind Speed  (m/s) | Average wind speed; helps fire spread |
| Burning Index  (Index)  | Fire danger index (measures fire intensity and difficulty of suppresion) |
| 100-hour Fuel Moisture (%) | Moisture content of medium-sized fuels (responds to weather after ~100 hours). |
| 1000-hour Fuel Moisture (%) | Moisture content of large fuels, e.g. logs. (responds to weather after ~1,000 hours). |
| Energy Release Component (Index)  | Composite index describing potential heat output from a fire; increases fire intensity. |
| Reference Evapotranspiration (mm/day) | Estimated water loss from a reference surface. |
| Potential Evapotranspiration (mm/day) | Maximum possible evapotranspiration (how dry the environment can become) |
| Vapor Pressure Deficit (kPa) | Difference between actual and saturated vapor pressure (correlated to air dryness). |
| Latitude | The geographic latitude of the ignition event. |
| Longitude | The geographic longitude of the ignition event. |
| Date    | The date each observation describes. |

: {.striped .hover}

:::

# Data Exploration

# Inference & Modeling

## Predicting Burning Index (BI)

- **Response variable:** Burning Index (BI) — a measure of wildfire difficulty
- **Predictors:** Environmental variables from GRIDMET
  - Excluded Energy release component (ERC), which is used to compute BI, and Evapotranspiration (feeds into ERC)
- **Goal:** Predict BI from daily meteorological conditions

## Step 1 — LASSO for Variable Selection

- Fit an initial LASSO regression across all possible predictors
- Examined strip plots of coefficients to assess variable influence
- Variables who clustered near zero means they have low influence, so removed them from final variable selection
- LASSO shrinkage gave us a clean first pass at which variables matter

## Step 2 — Checking Multicollinearity

- Environmental variables are often correlated, such as temperature & humidity
- Ran Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) tests on LASSO-selected variables
- Fine-tuned variable selection and ran VIF tests until variables were ideally <= 5
- This ensures our final model coefficients are stable and still be understandable without the influence of highly correlated predictors inflating variance

 

## Final Selected Variables

- Longitude
- Latitude
- Solar radiation (srad)
- Fuel moisture indices (fm100)
- Vapor pressure deficit (vpd)
- Wind speed (vs)
- Precipitation (pr)
- Specific humidity (sph)

## Step 3 — OLS vs. Ridge vs. LASSO

After finalizing variables, we compared three linear models:

| Model | R-squared | RMSE | Mean Absolute Error | 
|-------|----|------|-------|
| OLS   | 0.7266 | 12.9881 | 9.0110 | 
| Ridge | 0.7266 | 12.9882 | 9.0114 | 
| LASSO | 0.7266 | 12.9868 | 9.0108 | 


All of our linear regression models perform nearly identically, which suggests our selected variables are overall well suited and regularization offers little additional benefit here.


## Step 4 — Neural Network

We fit a foward neural network to compare results to our linear regression models

- Activation: ReLU at each hidden layer
- Trained up to 100 epochs

## Diagram of Neural Network
![](neuralnetwork_diagram.jpg){fig-align="center" width=120% height=500px}

## Neural Network Results

| Model | Test R-squared | RMSE | Mean Absolute Error | 
|-------|----|------|-------|
| Neural Network   | 0.9653 | 4.6238 | 3.0590 | 

- R-squared jumped from 0.73 to 0.96 over linear models

# Discussion

## Training Curve of the Neural Network
![](training_curve.png){fig-align="center" width=85% height=350px}

::: {style="font-size: 0.7em"}
- Loss drops sharply in the first ~10 epochs, capturing most signal quickly
- Train and validation loss track closely, which shows no sign of overfitting
- After ~50 epochs, improvement begins to plateau
- Running to 100 epochs was likely unnecessary — ~50 epochs may suffice
:::

## Model Comparison

| Model | Train R-squared | Test R-squared | RMSE | MAE |
|-------|----------|---------|------|-----|
| OLS | 0.7165 | 0.7266 | 12.9881 | 9.0110 |
| Ridge | 0.7165 | 0.7266 | 12.9882 | 9.0114 |
| LASSO | 0.7165 | 0.7266 | 12.9868 | 9.0108 |
| Neural Network | 0.9686 | 0.9653 | 4.6238 | 3.0590 |

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em;"}
- NN explained 23.87% more variance, which is a meaningful jump in predictive power, as mentioned in the prior slide
- RMSE cut nearly in third compared to linear models
- The non-linear (ReLU) relationships in climate data may help capture patterns that linear models cannot
:::

## Interpretation

- The linear regression models (OLS, Ridge, LASSO) explain approximately 72.66% of the variance in Burning Index (R-squared = 0.7266), suggesting a moderately strong linear relationship between the meteorological predictors and Burning Index
- The Neural 's ability to learn non-linear (ReLU) relationships likely explains the substantial performance gap, raising explained variance to 96.53%
- Similar train/test R-squared (0.9686 vs 0.9653) suggests the model generalizes well to unseen data across the US wildfires in 2024


## Limitations
- We predict Burning Index, not actual ignition. A high BI day does not guarantee a wildfire
- We used a 100 epoch ceiling rather than early stopping. Using ~50 epochs likely would have been sufficient based on the training curve
- Our model treats each day independently and does not explicitly model the 75-day sequence window in the dataset
  
## Conclusion

- With more time, we'd explore early stopping in our neural network and leverage the full 75-day time frame into our models. 
- Overall, the meteorological variables we used are moderately strong predictors.

## Thank You
